     -. Fig. 68: Haemochromogen crystals                         Fig. 69: Haemin crystal

second degree, but in a more severe form. The burnt
part is completely charred.
     Clinical classification (1) Superficial, when less
than full thickness of skin is involved. (2) Deep.
     Effects: The effects depend on: (1) The degree of
heat : The effects are severe, if the heat applied is very
great. The body of an adult does not burn completely
in a burnt house, as the temperature usually does not
exceed 650°C. For purpose of cremation, a human
body has to be incinerated for one-and-half hours at
1000°C. The ashes weigh two to three kg., and contain
bone fragments which can be identified as human. (2)
The duration of exposure : The symptoms are more                              9     9
severe if the heat is applied for a long time. (3) The
extent of the surface : The estimation of the surface
area of the body involved is usually worked out by
the "rule of nine" (Wallace); 9% for the head and
neck (head 7%, neck 2% ); 9% for each upper limb;
(arm 4%, forearm 3%, hand 2%), 9% for the front of
each lower limb; 9% for the back of each lower limb;          Fig. (11-2). Estimation of the extent of surface burns.
(thigh 10%, leg 5%, foot 3% for each lower limb); 9%
for the front of chest; 9% for the back of chest; 9%
for the front of the abdomen; and 9% for the back of
abdomen, 99% of the body. The remaining one percent
is for the external genitalia (Fig.11-2 & 11-3). Palmar
surface of patient's hand is about one percent of
body surface area. Involvement of 50 percent of the
body surface will prove fatal even when the burns are
only of the first degree. Skin surface is about 4.6 sq.
metres in an adult. (4) The site: Burns of the head and
neck, trunk or the anterior abdominal wall are more
dangerous. (5) Age: Children are more susceptible,
old people less. (6) Sex: Women are more susceptible.
     Local effects of thermal injury include: vascular
thrombosis and tissue necrosis, increase evaporation
of water from burnt surface and local heat loss.
     Causes of Death : (1) Primary (neurogenic)
shock due to pain, etc. (2) More than half of deaths
from burns occur within the first 48 hours usually               Fig. (11-3). Estimation of the extent of surface
from secondary shock, due to fluid loss from burned                             burns in children.
surface. Circulatory collapse may occur with 15%
of burns of total body surface area. (3) In smoke            important factor in deaths occurring four to five days
inhalation apart from CO, the other factors which            or longer after burning. (6) Biochemical disturbances,
contribute to death are oxygen deprivation, cyanide,         secondary to the fluid loss and destruction of tissue,
free radicals (inactivate surfactants, thus preventing       e.g., hypokalaemia. (7) Acute renal failure, due
oxygen from crossing the alveoli into the blood), and        to lower nephron nephrosis occurs on the third or
non-specific toxic substances. (4) Toxaemia, due             fourth day. (8) Gastrointestinal disturbances, such
to absorption of various metabolites from the burnt          as acute peptic ulceration, dilation of the stomach,
tissue persists up to 3 to 4 days. (5) Sepsis is the most    haemorrhage into intestines. (9) Oedema of glottis

## Starvation



     Starvation may occur from the actual withholding       by pain in the epigastrium which is relieved by
of food or from the administration of unsuitable food.      pressure. After four to five days of starvation. general
It is of two types: (1) Acute, and (2) Chronic. Acute       emaciation and absorption of the subcutaneous fat
 tarvation results from sudden and complete stoppage        begins to occur. The eyes are sunken and glistening;
of food. Chronic starvation results from gradual            the pupils are dilated; the cheeks sink and the bony
deficient supply of food. Starvation deaths may be          prominences become visible. Bichat's buccal pad of
due to (1) famine, (2) being trapped in pits, mines         fat is among the last subcutaneous adipose tissue to
or landslides, (3) neglect on the part of the parents       disappear. The lips are dry and cracked, and the tongue
or guardians. (4) wilful withholding of food. and (5)       coated and dirty, and thirst is intolerable. The sali va is
wilful refusal to take food.                                thick and scanty. The voice is weak and whispering.
     Types: (I) Acute. (2) Chronic.                         The skin is dry, rough, thin, inelastic, wrinkled and
     Symptoms: In acute starvation, there is a feeling      pigmented. Emaciation may be extreme, the abdomen
of hunger for the first 30 to 48 hours, which is followed   concave and limbs become thin and flaccid with loss
                                                            of muscular power. Muscular weakness is progressive
                                                            and may be severe. Cardiovascular changes are those
                                                            of progressive insufficiency. The pulse is slow at rest,
                                                            but on exertion paroxysmal tachycardia supervenes.
                                                            The temperature is subnormal. Constipation is
                                                            usual, but towards death diarrhoea and dysentery
                                                            are common. The urine is scanty, turbid, and highl y
                                                            concentrated and shows evidence of acidosis.The
                                                            loss of weight is most marked and constant. In the
                                                            last stage, the body is reduced to an extreme state of
                                                            emaciation. The ribs are prominent, with concavities
                                                            in the intercostal spaces and sunken supraclavicular
                                                            fossae. Before death the body has an offensive odour.
                                                            Death usually occurs when 40% of the 01iginal weight
                                                            is reached. The intellect remains clear till death.
                                                            though in some cases delusions and hallucinati ons of
                                                            sight and hearing occur.
                                                                 As starvation continues, at first rapid mobilisation
                 Fig. (12-1). Starvation.                   of protein stores occur which are converted by the liver

to glucose, which is mainly used to supply energy to the            absent in the subcutaneous tissues and also in the omentum,
brain. After that, the main means of energy production              mesentery and about the internal organs, which is never seen
is lipolysis. Adipose tissue releases free fatty acids. In the      in wasting disease. The fat of the female breast and of the
liver they are the substrate for synthesis of ketone bodies,        orbit is spared till late. Subepicardial fat becomes replaced
which are used as fuels for skeletal and heart muscle and           by a watery gelatinous material. In children, the skeleton
brain. As complete depletion of fat stores approaches,
                                                                    shows spinal curvature, rickets and dental defects. In adults
protein is again rapidly used as a source of energy.
                                                                    progressive demineralisation and osteomalacia are seen.

constant and develop in a constant order. (1) Loss of
                                                                    changes similar to premature senility. There is extreme
well-being, hunger and hunger-pains. (2) Mental and
                                                                    emaciation and general reduction in size and weight of all
physical lethargy and easy fatigue. (3) Progressive loss of
weight which is rapid in the first six months. (4) Polyuria.        the organs except the brain, which is sometimes pale and
(5) Increasing cachexia, the body weight is reduced by              soft. Muscles are atrophied and darker due to increase in
about 40% of the normal. Pigmentation and anaemia.                  lipochrome. The fibres lose striations and become more
(6) Hypothermia, peripheral vascular. stasis in cold                uniform from granular degeneration. The heart is small
and hypotension. (7) Extreme lethargy, gross mental                 from brown atrophy and the chambers are empty. The lungs
retardation and loss of self-respect. (8) Oedema, first             are pale and collapsed, and exude very little blood when
in the feet and lower Limbs. (9) Reduced resistance to              cut. Rarely, there may be oedema and hypostatic basal
infection causing diarrhoea, dysentery, tuberculosis, etc.          congestion. The stomach and intestines show atrophy of
     The blood sugar, proteins, chlorides and cholesterol           all coats and the mucosa is stained with bile. The walls of
are lowered. Non-protein nitrogen and urea plasma free
                                                                    intestine appear like tissue paper with atrophy of mucosa.
acids and ketone bodies are raised.                                 The bowel contains offensive watery fluid and gas. There
     Cause of Death : Death occurs from exhaustion,                 may be superficial but extensive non-specific ulceration of
circulatory failure due to brown atrophy of the                     the bowel like those seen in ulcerative colitis. The liver is
heart, or intercurrent infection. Dehydration and                   atrophied and may show necrosis due to protein deficiency.
hypothermia contribute to death.                                    Spleen is shrunken. The gall bladder is distended with bile.
     Fatal Period : If both water and food are                      The kidneys show atrophy of the nephron. Blood volume
completely withdrawn death occurs in 10 to 12 days.                 is markedly reduced, and there is marked anaemia. The
If food alone is withdrawn death occurs in 6 to 8 weeks             urinary bladder is empty. There may be evidence of some
or even more. Death usually occurs when about 70 to                 intercurrent disease.
90% of body fat, and 20% of body protein are lost.                       Medicolegal Aspects : The exclusion of disease

Combined asphyxia and venous congestion : This                (Fig. 13-2). (2) Width and multiplicity of ligature:
is the commonest cause. (4) Cerebral anaemia: If
the knot is situated in the region of nape of neck, both
arterial and venous blood flow are cut off by pressure
of the noose on both sides of neck. Pressure on the
large arteries on the neck produces cerebral anaemia
and immediate coma (10 to 15 seconds). If pressure
is immediately released, consciousness is regained in
 10 to 12 seconds. Death will be slow and asphyxial
features are less marked. This occurs with ligature
made of thin cord, which sinks deeply into tissues.
A tension of 4 to 5 kg. on ligature blocks carotid
arteries, and 20 kg. the vertebral arteries. (5) Reflex
vagal inhibition from pressure on the vagal sheath
or carotid bodies. Fear, apprehension, struggling and
possibly alcohol, may heighten the sensitivity of vagal
mechanism. The release of catecholamines during
such .adrenal response may sensitise the myocardium
to such neurogenic stimulation. (6) Fracture or                        Fig. (13-2). Ligature mark of hanging
dislocation of the cervical vertebrae. This occurs                            showing pattern of chain.

Position of the knot: The main force applied to the
neck by ligature is opposite to the point of suspension.
If the point of suspension is in occipital region, front
of the neck is involved. If in front, the depth of the
groove is limited posteriorly by cervical spine. (7)
Slipping of ligature during suspension: Frequently,
only the portion adjacent to the knot moves. There
is a tendency for the ligature to move upwards, this
being limited by the jaws. The upward movement
may produce double impression of ligature. The lower
mark is usually very superficial and is connected by
                                                              Fig. (13-3). Method of preservation of noose in hanging
fine abrasions, caused by the slipping ligature, to the                         and strangulation.
mark made by ligature in its final position.
     Knot: A slip knot (running noose) is produced
by attaching a rope to itself, creating a loop which can
be tightened to produce a running noose. A binding
knot is a knot in which rope passes at least once
around the neck, and is held in place by two ends of
the rope being knotted together. This produces a fixed
noose. Reef knot is a flat knot used in tying reef-
points consisting of two loops passing symmetrically
through each other that will not slip. Granny knot
is knot like reef knot, but asymnetrical , apt to slip or
jam. NOose is a loop with running knot which ties the               Loop              Slip-knot              Fixed loop
firmer the closer it is drawn. It is frequently in the form                Fig. ( 1~) . Types of ligature.

                                                             back of the neck. (3) In the case of a fixed loop with
                                                             a single knot in the midline under the chin, the mark

                                                                 William Lynch, who used to order hanging on the spot

                                                                 to teaching others a lesson for committing offence. It

particularly on the thighs. (Fig. 13-19). It is produced    occurs due to absorption of water into its outer layer.
by the spasm of the erector pilae muscles, attached to      It is first seen on the fingertips in two to four hours
                                                            and spreads to the palm and the backs of the fingers,
                                                            and the back of the hand, in that order in about
                                                            twentyfour hours. Similar progress and changes are
                                                            seen in the skin of the foot, but when shoes are worn,
                                                            it takes almost twice as long. Wrinkling of the skin
                                                            begins to appear shortly after immersion, bleaching
                                                            of epidermis in four to eight hours, and the bleached,
                                                            wrinkled and sodden appearance is seen in twenty
                                                            hours. The skin becomes sodden, thickened, wrinkled,
                                                            and white in colour, known as "washerwoman's
                                                            hands". (Fig. 13-20) This is seen early in warm
                                                            water. The accuracy of timing is not possible due
                                                            to variations in environmental conditions. Similar
                                                            changes are seen on the feet.
                                                                 Contusions and abrasions produced during life
                                                            may not be seen after removal from water, but are
                                                            seen after the drying of the skin. In a few hours after
                                                            removal from the water, the face may be bloated,
                                                            either livid or black, later changing to a deep green.

drowned persons shows distension of alveoli, alveolar     results in dilation of the right side of the heart and

been rtptured. Capillary congestion, intra-alveolar       endothelium of pulmonary capillaries. The intima of
haemorrhages and protein-1ich oedema fluid are often      the aorta is stained red.

Stamach: The stomach contains water in 70% of
cases. If the chemical and microscopic nature of the
water is same as that of the medium of submersion, it
is a valuable confirmatory evidence of drowning, but
it is possible that the victim might have drunk the same
water shortly before death. When a disagreeable liquid
which would not be swallowed voluntarily and which
corresponds to the drowning medium, e.g. , liquid
manure, or muddy water containing debris is found in
the stomach, it is a valuable indication of drowning.
The amount should be measured and examined for
foreign substances. Water is also not found in the
stomach, if the person died from syncope or shock
and in putrefaction. Gastric mucosa is often soft and
heavy. The small intestine may contain water in about
20% cases. This sign is regarded as positive evidence
of death by drowning as it depends on peristaltic
movement which is a vital act.
      The brain is cogested, swollen with flattening of

## Impotence And Sterility



     Impotence is the inability of a person to            impotence). (2) Diabetes (when complicated by
perform sexual intercourse. Sterility is the inability    peripheral neuropathy). (3) Trauma; head injury and
of the male to beget children, and in the female the      spinal injury. (4) Venereal disease (syphilis in its
inability to conceive children. About 10 to 15% of        tertiary stage can affect the posterior column of spinal
all married couples are involuntarily sterile. A person   cord which can cause impotence). (5) Hypertension
can be sterile without being impotent, or he can be       (ganglion blocking drugs can cause impotence).
impotent without being sterile, or both may co-exist.     (6) Occupation (painters, compositors and workers

## Sterility In The Male

                                                           etc. Diseases of the testicles, epididymis or penis, such
    (1) Age : The power of erection and therefore          as cancer, sarcoma, tuberculosis, syphilis, trauma,
of coitus, may be present at a much earlier age than       etc., may cause sterility, impotence or both. Fracture

is a common cause of impotence, and therefore              at L-4 and 5 level with injury to sacral segments

development of the private parts of the person than        genito-urinary nerve) and brain damage can result in
on his age. In cases of precocious development, as         impotence. Bilateral lumbar sympathectomy causes
in gonadal or adrenal tumours, Me Cune Albright            impotence. Tumours or injury of cauda equina, and
 yndrome, the sexual organs may show advance               spina bifida produce impotence. Lithotomy operation
development as compared to the body as a whole. In         may damage ejaculatory ducts and produce sterility.
advanced age, the power of erection and the ability        Exposure to X-rays causes temporary azoospermia.
to perform the coitus may diminish or disappear, but       When spermatic cords are blocked due to operation
there is no specific age at which such loss of power       or disease, ligated or cut, sterility results.
occurs. Spermatozoa are not usually found before the            (4) General Diseases : Impotence is common

diabetes, pulmonary tuberculosis, chronic nephritis,       blood flow (arteriogenic impotence), or excessive
etc., may cause temporary impotence. General ill-          venous leakage of blood from the penis which can
health may be associated with diminished fertility.        occur in adult life after years of normal sexual life or
Endocrine disease may produce sexual infantalism           due to congenital anomalies (venogenic impotence)
and impotence. Certain conditions of the central           or both. Other causes are diabetes mellitus (20% ),
nervous system, such as hemiplegia, paraplegia,            psychogenic (12%), neurogenic (7%), due to injuries
syringomyelia, locomotor ataxia, disseminated              to the spine, pelvic or perirenal injuries and pelvic
sclerosis, may cause impotence, but this is not always     surgeries causing injury to the nerves of the penis,
so. Lack of sexual power is common in those suffering      endocrinologic, such as testosterone deficiency,
from paranoia, tabes dorsalis and general paralysis        luteinising hormone and prolactin, hypothyroidism,
of the insane. Occasionally, the reverse effect, i.e.      Cushing's syndrome, trauma, etc. (20% ).
satyriasis or exessive sexual desire is seen. Impotence         DIAGNOSTIC PROCEDURES: For the functional

 CTIONS (Braxton-Hick's sign) : Intermittent, painless         gonadotropin as the antigen and antiserum to HCG. A
 uterine contractions are difficult to be observed before
 the third month, but are easily felt after the fourth
 month. Each contraction lasts about a minute and

      (5) BALLOTTEMENT : It means to toss up like a
 ball. This is positive during the fourth and fifth months
 of pregnancy as the foetus is small in relation to the
 amount of amniotic fluid present. To obtain vaginal
 ballottement, two fingers are inserted into the anterior
 fornix and a sudden upward motion given. This causes
 the foetus to move up in the liquor amnii and after a
 moment, the foetus drops down on the fingers like a ball
 bouncing back. External ballottement can be obtained
 by imparting a sudden motion to the abdominal wall
 covering the uterus; in a few seconds the rebound of the
 foetus can be felt. This can be negative if the amniotic
 fluid is scanty.

 (5)   Cavity:   The walls are concave from inside forming a        The walls are convex from inside forming
                 wider and rounded cavity.                          smaller and triangular cavity.
 C6)   Cervix:   Irregular, and edges show scars. External os       Regular, no scars. External os is rounded and
                 is transverse, irregular, and fissured. It may     appears as a small hollow in the centre of cervix.
                 admit tip of the finger; the internal os is not    The mucous membrane is smooth and intact and
                 well-defined.                                      orifice closed. Internal os circular, well-defined.

the witnesses. in most cases it is not possible to estimate
the intenal between instrumentation and death.
     (2) WHAT KIND OF INSTRUMENT WAS USED?
The nature of the injuries may indicate whether the
instrument is penetrating or non-penetrating and
sharp-pointed, blunt-pointed, hard-nosed, or soft-nosed,
but the exact nature cannot be made out. Injecting
instruments usually do not produce injuries. Marks
produced by surgical forceps, tenaculum, etc., during
treatment may cause confusion. The doctor who
attended on the patient should be shown the specimen
to exclude any marks made by him.
     (3) HOW MAY ANY INJURIES PRESENT BE
INTERPRETED?

interference, i.e., dilating, perforating, injecting, etc.,                                      Cervical necrosis

supervene in areas of bruising and internal laceration
of the cervix or uterus, which may cause difficulty in
interpretation.                                                  Fig. (17-4). Complications of criminal abortion.

    Trait              Before respiration                              After respiration
 (1) Weight:            1/70 of body weight.                           1/35 of body weight.
 (2) Volume:           Normal or small.                                Larger, and cover the heart.

 (6) Air vesicles:     Not inflated.                                   Inflated.

 (8) Floatation:        Whole and parts sink in water                  Expanded areas or whole float in water.

THE ABANDONING OF INFANTS: (1) If the                     Parents tend to be young between 20 to 30 years, and
father or mother of a child under the age of twelve           belong to lower social class and lower education. The
years, or anyone having the care of such child,               family is usually isolated. There is often a history of
leaves such a child in any place with the intention           family disharmony, long-standing emotional problems
of abandoning the child, shall be punished with               or financial problems. Many of the fathers have
imprisonment up to seven years. (2) If the child so           criminal records, or unemployed or socially unstable.
exposed or abandoned is a girl child, the person doing        Many mothers have multiple social and psychiatric
so shall be punished with rigorous imprisonment for           problems with a chaotic and violent home background.
a term which may extend to ten years and shall also           The mother is of lower I.Q., often pregnant or in the
be liable to fine which may extend to one lakb rupees         premenstrual period at the time of battering. Unhappy
(Sec. 317, I.P.C.).                                           childhood experiences are common in both parents

    (VII) Artefacts in Firearm Wounds: ( 1) Drainage
wounds may be mistaken for firearm wounds. (2) In
decomposition, there may be peeling of skin and

entrance wound from exit. (3) Cleaning, excision,
suturing or surgical extension of entry wound will
remove gunpowder and alter size, shape, etc. causing
difficulty in differentiating entry and exit wound. (4)

         Preparation                         Toxic substance
(A)      Domestic Poisons :
(I)      Cosmetics:
         (1) Cuticle remover:                Potassium hydroxide; trisodium phosphate.
         (2) Depilatories :                  Barium sulphide; thallium.
         (3) Hair wave lotions :             Thioglycollate salts; perborates; bromates.
         (4) Nail polish removers:           Acetone.
        · (5) Suntan lotions :               Denatured alcohol; methyl salicylate.
         (6) Baby powder :                   Boric acid.
(II)     Kitchen:
         (1) Baking powder :                 Tartaric acid 50%.
         (2) Baking soda :                   Sodium bicarbonate.
         (3) Dish washing compounds :        Sodium polyphosphates; sodium carbonate; sodium silicates.
         (4) Fire extinguishing fluids :     Carbon tetrachloride; sodium carbonate; methyl bromide.
         (5) Matches :                       Antimony; phosphorus; sesqui-sulphide, potassium chlorate.
(III)    Rat Poisons :
         (1) Rat paste :                     Aluminium phosphide; zinc sulphide; zinc phosphide;
                                             arsenious oxide; red squill; thallium sulphate, phosphorus,

         (2) Rodine:                         Yell ow phosphorus.
(IV)     Roach powder :                      Sodium fluoride.
(V)      Sanitary :
         (1) Deodorant tablets :             Formaldehyde; naphthalene.
         (2) Drain cleaners :                Sodium hydroxide.
         (3) Disinfectants :                 Phenol; bleaching powder (calcium hydrochlorite).
(VI)     Miscellaneous :
         (1) Insecticide spray :             D.D.T., Gammexane, etc.
         (2) Moth balls :                    Naphthalene.
         (3) Marking ink :                   Aniline.
         (4) Ink remover:                    Sodium hypochlorite 5%
         (5) Anti-rust products :            Ammonium sulphide; hydrofluoric acid; naphtha; oxalic acid.
         (6) Cleaning solvents:              Petroleum hydrocarbons; carbon tetrachloride; trichloroethylene.
         (7) Flourescent lamps :             Beryllium
         (8) Furniture polish :              Turpentine; petroleum hydrocarbons.
         (9) Paint remover :                 Sodium hydroxide; lead acetate.
         (10) Shoe polish :                  Aniline; nitrobenzene.
         (11) Hair bleach:                   Potassium permanganate; hydrogen peroxide.

avoid any injury when it is being passed. At about

(5) Lacrimal glands: Increased lacrimation. (6)            trichlorfon, etc. It begins with paraesthesias and pain
C.V.S.: Bradycardia or tachycardia, arrhythmias,           or cramps in the calves followed by ataxia, weakness,

occasionally unequal or dilated. (8) Ciliary body:         which can ascend similar to Guillain-Barre syndrome.

muscle: Initial stimulus results in contraction. Later          Fatal Dose:
there is paralysis due to persistent depolarisation.            TEPP 50 mg. i.m. or 100 mg. orally.

of respiratory and cardiovascular centres.                 which disappear in 2 to 3 days, but may sometimes

              NITRIC ACID (HNOJ)                              or black, due to the production of acid haematin.
    Nitric acid (aqua fortis; red spirit of nitre) is a       Inhalation of fumes causes intense irritation of throat
clear, colourless, fuming, heavy liquid, and has a
peculiar and choking odour. In concentrated form
it combines with organic matter and produces
an yellow discolouration of tissue due to the
production of picric acid (xanthoproteic reaction).
    Signs and Symptoms : They are those of
poisoning by sulphuric acid. There is more eructation
and greater abdominal distention owing to gas
formation. It causes yellow discolouration of the
tissues, including the crowns of the teeth and yellow
stains on the clothing. Inhalation of fumes causes
lachrymation, photophobia, irritation of air-passages
and lungs producing sneezing, coughing, dyspnoea
and asphyxia. Colour of urine is brown.
    Fatal Dose: 10 to 15 mi.
    Fatal Period: 12 to 24 hours.
    Postmortem Appearances: They are those of
sulphuric acid, but the tissues are stained yellow. In           Fig. (26-1). Corrosive acid burns with dribbling

barley, wheat, oats, etc. It gradually replaces the grain     or sips of ice-cold water.
forming a curved, dark-purple or black compact mass,              Criminal Uses: ( 1) Powder is thrown into the eyes
0.9 to one em. long, half em. thick, with lengthwise          to facilitate robbery . (2) When theft or confession of
ridges, and has peculiar odour and disagreeable taste.        some guilt has to be obtained, the person is tortured by
It contains about thirty alkaloids, but ergotoxine,

erogotamine and erogometrine (ecbolics) are important.

    Ergot alkaloids exert their primary effect by
stimulating adrenergic receptors, both peripherally and              SEMECARPUS ANACARDIUM
centrally. They directly stimulate muscle fibres.                              (see Plate 14, Fig. 83)

## Calotropis

    Calotropis gig an tea (akdo, madar) has purple
flowers and calotropis procera has white flowers. They
grow wild throughout India. The active principles
are uscharin, calotoxin, calactin and calotropin
(glycoside). The milky juice in addition contains
trypsin. The leaves and stem when incised yield thick
acrid, milky juice.
     Signs and Symptoms: Applied to the skin,
it causes redness and vesication. When taken by
mouth, the juice produces an acrid bitter taste, and
burning pain in throat and stomach, salivation,
stomatitis, vomiting, diarrhoea, dilated pupils, tetanic
convulsions, collapse and death.
    Fatal Dose: Uncertain.                                            Fig. (29-1). Plumbago zeylanica.

                                 Table (29-2). Difference between cobra and viper
       Trait               Cobra                                          Viper
 (1)   Body:               Long and cylindrical.                          Short, narrow neck.
 (2)   Head:               Small; seldom broader than body; covered       Large; broader than body; triangular
                           by large scales or shields of special forms.   and covered by numerous small scales.
 (3)   Pupil:              Round.                                         Vertical.
 (4)   Maxillary bone:     Carries poison fangs and other teeth.          Carries only poison fangs.
 (5)   Fangs:              Grooved, short, fine.                          Canalised, long.
 (6)   Venom:              Neurotoxic.                                    Haemotoxic.
 (7)   Tail:               Round.                                         Tapering.
 (8)   Eggs:               Lay eggs.                                      Give birth to young ones.

                                    Table (30-2). Physiological effects of alcohol
 Blood alcohol concentration      Effects
 0 to SO mg %                     No significant effect or mild euphoria.
 SO to 100 mg%                    Decreased inhibitions, increased self-confidence, decreased attention span,
                                  slurring of speech, mild incoordination, alteration of judgement, nystagmus.
 100 to 1SO rug %                 Some mental confusion, emotional instability, loss of critical judgement, ataxia,
                                  impaired memory, sleepiness, slowed reaction time.
 ISO to 300 rug%                  Loss of muscular coordination, staggering gait, marked mental confusion,
                                  drowsiness, exaggeration of emotions, dizziness, decreased pain response,
                                  disorientation; thickened speech.
 300 to 400 rug%                  Stupor, marked incoordination, marked decrease in responses to stimuli,
                                  possibly coma.
 406 rug % & above                Anaesthesia, depression of responses, respiratory failure, deep coma, death.

## Psychotropic Drugs



     Psychotropic drugs are substances that affect              depressant effects on the heart by altering sodium
 psychic function, behaviour or experience. They                influx resulting in conduction delays and myocardial
 consist of: (1) Antidepressants. (2) Neuroleptics. (3)         depression, (4) peripheral alpha blockade causing
 Hallucinogens.                                                 hypotension, and (5) inhibition of sympathetic reflexes
     Psychoactive Drug Classification: (1) Sedatives:           centrally.
                                                                    STIMULANTS: They include cocaine,
 (a) Barbiturates and others. (b) Minor tranquilisers.

 methylphenidate. (b) Cocaine. (3) Opiates: Heroin,             sense of well-being, increase alertness, decrease fatigue,
 methadone, morphine, etc. (4) Hallucinogens. (5)               suppress appetite and can lead to strong psychological
 Marihuana (6) Major tranquilisers: chlorpromazine              dependence.
 and others. (7) Antidepressants : (a) Tricyclics. (b)                     METHAMPHETAMINE
 Monoamine oxidase inhibitors. (8) Antimania drugs.                 Action: Amphetamines increase the synaptic

They are highly protein-bound and have good CNS                with varied perceptual abnormalities and varied
penetration. Excretion in the urine may continue for           sensations. Sensory perception and thought processes
several days . Addiction may occur. They enhance               are grossly distorted, and mood is altered. Reality (space,
the inhibitory actions of the neurotransmitter GABA,           time, bodily dimensions) is distorted and a feeling of
located in the brain.                                          depersonalisation makes the patient feel separated from
     Fatal Dose: 100 to 300 mg/kg body weight. Death           the situation. Hallucinations produced by drugs usually

is rare.
                                                               for the illusion. Most of the hallucinations are visual.
     Signs and Symptoms: Symptoms appear in 1 to 3             Often hallucinations take the form of gross somatic
hours. Acute poisoning causes vertigo, slurred speech,         distortions. Occasionally blending of sensory modalities
nystagmus, diplopia, dysarthria, ataxia, staggering            (synesthesia) may be noted. Profound philosophical
walk, shallow breathing, sedation and somnolence and           thoughts, deep introspection, self-examination, or
coma. If taken alone they are not toxic, but mixed with        religious overtones may be prominent components of the

     ALKALOIDS: An alkaloid is a complex substance             pain in the stomach and vomiting are fust noticed.
having a nitrogenous base, and is found in various plants.     The voice becomes hoarse. The face becomes flushed,
Chemically, it behaves like an alkali in that it unites        conjunctivae congested, pupils widely dilated with
with acids to form salts. Its basic quality depends on the     loss of accommodation for near vision, developing in
pyridine nucleus. In nature they are usually combined          temporary blindness, photophobia and diplopia. Light
with certain acids to form salts. In plants they are not       reflex at first is sluggish and later absent. The pollen
uniformly distributed but are concentrated in different
structures, such as the root, bark, leaves, or seeds, which
vary with the species. They act mainly on some portions
of the central nervous system, each compound having its
own individual action. Some of the important alkaloids
are atropine, hyoscine, morphine, quinine, strychnine,
aconitine, ergotoxine, cocaine, and codeine. The toxicity
of different alkaloids varies greatly, aconitine bieng 1,000
times as toxic as quinine. Some synthetic substances, such
as amphetamine, heroin, pethidine, methadone, also
behave chemically like alkaloids. Some alkaloids, e.g.
strychnine, morphine and aconitine are quite resistant to
putrefaction, while others, e.g. cocaine decompose rapidly.          Datura seeds                   Capsicum seeds

distortion of perception to actual hallucinations. The
person feels powerful dreams, heightened sensation
and detachment from reality . The sufferer often
behaves totally irrationally, commits antisocial acts
and may injure or even kill himself. Later, the abuser
will often have complete amnesia for the period of
intoxication. Most of the abusers are young, usually
male teenagers. There is no physical withdrawal
syndrome.
     Cause of Death: (1) The major cause of death is
due to sudden cardiac arrest, following an arrhythmia.
Any sudden "flight or fright" stimulus, even some
considerable time after sniffing, has the ability to
precipitate ventricular fibrillation and sudden death.

## Spinal Poisons



         STRYCHNOS NUX VOMICA                               strychnine from the liver and muscles produces
                (see Plate 14, Fig. 82)                     convulsions on second or third day of poisoning,
     Strychnine ( kuchila) is a powerful alkaloid           after sedation is discontinued. About 80% is oxidised
obtained from the seeds of strychnos nux vomica,            mainly in the liver. It is excreted slowly by the kidneys
and other species of strychnos, which are found in the      and traces in bile, milk and saliva. It may be found in
jungles in India. Fruit is round, hard, slightly rough,     the cadaver up to four years.
glossy-orange, 4 to 5 em. wide, with jelly-like white            Action: It competitively blocks ventral horn
or pale yellow pulp. It has 3 to 5 seeds. The seeds of      motor neurone postganglionic receptor sites in the
nux vomica contained in the ripe fruit are poisonous.       spinal cord and brainstem and prevents the effects
The seeds contain two principal alkaloids; strychnine       of glycine (the presumed inhibitory transmitter).
and brucine one-and-half percent each. The seeds            Widespread inhibition in the spinal cord results in
also contain a glucoside, loganin. Strychnine occurs        'reiease' excitation. The action is particularly noted
as colourless, odourless, rhombic prisms, having an         in the anterior horn cells. It stimulates the cerebral
intensely bitter taste. The bark contains only brucine.
The fruit pulp has very low strychnine content.
All parts of the tree are toxic. Brucine is allied to
strychnine in composition and action. Strychnine is
10 to 20 times more poisonous than brucine.
    The seeds are flat, circular discs or slightly convex
on one side, concave on the other, two-and-half em.
in diameter, 6 mm. in thickness. They are ash-grey
or light-brown in colour, have a shining surface and
covered with radiating silky fibres. They are very hard,
tough and difficult to pulverize. The bark, wood and
leaves contain brucine but no strychnine. Strychnine
is used as a respiratory stimulant, as a rodenticide and
for killing stray dogs.
    Absorption and Excretion: All mucous
membrane~ absorb strychnine. Much is taken up
by the liver and muscles to be either released again
to blood stream or to be destroyed. The release of                   Fig. (34-1). Strychnos nux vomica.

cortex. GABA, the neurotransmitter for presynaptic          two minutes. In between the convulsions the muscles
 inhibitory neurons is not affected.                         are completely relaxed, and the patient looks well
      Signs and Symptoms: If swallowed oncrushed,            though somewhat exhausted, and the breathing is
 the seeds of nux vomica have no poisonous action, as        resumed. The cyanosis lessens, cold perspirations
 they are not dissolved in the gastrointestinal tract,       cover the skin; dilated pupils may contract. After 5 to
 and are passed entire in the faeces. When crushed           15 minutes or on slightest impulse, e.g. a sudden noise,
 seeds are taken, the symptoms are delayed for an hour       a current of air, or gently touching the patient, another
 or more. If the alkaloid is swallowed, the symptoms         convulsion occurs. In fatal cases, the convulsions
 occur very rapidly, usually within five to fifteen          rapidly succeed one another, and increase in severity
 minutes. Bitter taste in the mouth, sense of uneasiness     and in duration, and death usually occurs after four
 and restlessness, feeling of suffocation and fear, and      to five convulsions. The patient cannot breathe
 difficulty in swallowing occur. The convulsions are         because the diaphragm and thoracic muscles are fully
 preceded by such prodromal symptoms as increased            contracted. Hypoxia causes medullary paralysis and
 acuity of perception, increased rigidity of muscles,        death. In non-fatal cases the intervals between the
 and muscular twitchings. Convulsions are produced           convulsions become longer and the spasm less, until
 due to direct action on the reflex centres of spinal        these entirely stop within 12 to 24 hours, and recovery
 cord, and affect all the muscles at a time. These are       takes place in a day or two.
 at first clonic, but eventually become tonic. During            Fatal Dose: 50 to 100 mg; one crushed seed.
 the convulsions, the face is cyanosed and has anxious           Fatal Period: One to two hours.
 look, eyes are staring, eyeballs prominent and pupils           Treatment: (1) The first step is the effective

 of the jaws and facial muscles in which the corners of      before the disease. The patient should be kept in a dark

 typically arches in hyperextension (opisthotonos). In       these prove ineffective consider general anaesthesia

taste. (2) Suicide is rare because of the painful death.

overdose of medicinal preparation, or the poison being
given by mistake, or in children by eating the seeds.           CONIUM MACULATUM (HEMLOCK)
(4) Sometimes, the seeds are used for killing the cattle,         The plant contains coniine and seven other alkaloids.
and as arrow poison. (5) Sometimes, it is taken as an         Coniine content is highest in the unripe fruit and in the
aphrodisiac.                                                  seeds, in the leaves especially at flowering time and in
                                                              the root particularly during the summer. Hemlock was
      PERIPHERAL NERVE POISONS                                administered to Socrates, the Greek Philosopher in 399
    CURARE: This is found in various species of               B.C. as a form of execution.
strychnos. Curarine is the active principle. Its action           Fatal Dose: 60 mg, of coniine
is entirely peripheral and at the myoneural junction,             SIGNS AND SYMPTOMS: The fresh leaves
and blocks the postsynaptic nicotinic acetylcholine           have a nauseating taste and unpleasant mousy odour.
receptors in muscles, thus causing a flaccid paralysis of     The odour of the dried leaves is strong and narcotic.
skeletal muscles. It is used in the production of muscular    Ingestion causes burning in mouth and throat, gastric
relaxation in patients who are lightly anaesthetised. It is   inflammation, vomiting, diarrhoea, slow respiration,
not poisonous when swallowed. It is absorbed through          increased and later slow pulse, mental confusion,
wounds or abrasions. It is used as arrow poison.              tremors, ataxia, sometimes blindness, progressive motor
    SIGNS AND SYMPTOMS: It causes gradual                     paralysis extending upwards from the extremities, coma
paralysis of limbs followed by paralysis of respiratory       and death from respiratory paralysis.

            NICOTIANA TABACUM:                                amblyopia, blindness, irregularity of the heart with

AU parts are poisonous except the ripe seeds. The dried       angina pectoris.

of nicotine and are used in the form of smoke or snuff        smoke, anxiety, impaired concentration and memory,
or chewed. The leaves contain active principles, which        depression or hostility, headache, muscle cramps,
are the toxic alkaloids nicotine and anabasine (which         sleep disturbances, increased appetite and weight gain,
are equally toxic); nornicotine (less toxic). Nicotine is a   diaphoresis and rapid respirations. A short period (6 to
colourless, volatile, bitter, hygroscopic liquid alkaloid.    12 weeks) of maintenance often followed by a gradual

smoking and application of leaves or juice to wound

## Digitalis Purpurea

     Entire plant is toxic, containing over thirty cardiac
and steroidal glucosides. The root, leaves and seeds
of digitalis contain digitoxin, digoxin, digitalin and
digitonin (glycosides).
     SIGNS AND SYMPTOMS: GIT: Anorexia,
nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea. CARDIAC: Arrhythmias:
extrasystoles, ventricular tachycardia and fibrillation,
atrial flutter and fibrillation, SA· block, AV block.
ENDOCRINE: Gynaecomastia. VISUAL: Transient
amblyopia, photophobia, diplopia, blurring, scotomata,
colour aberration, halos. SKIN: Urticaria. CNS:
Headache, fatigue, muscle weakness, neuro-psychiatric
disorders, confusion, anxiety, depression, disorientation,              Fig. (35-1). Digitalis purpurea.
drowsiness, delirium, hallucinations, trigeminal
neuralgia. Death occurs from cardiovascular collapse.

an abortifacient, root is used either locally or taken         Stomach and duodenum may be congested and may

venereal diseases. (5) Root is used for treating cancers           The Circumstances of Poisoning: (1) The root
and ulcers in the form of paste. (6) The decoction of          and seeds are used sometimes for suicide or homicide.
leaves is applied externally to reduce swelling. (7) As        (2) Root and seeds are taken for criminal abortion. (3)
a cattle poison, the juice of root is applied on piece         For cattle poisoning, the seeds are crushed and fed to
of cloth and inserted into the anus of the animal. (8)         the animal with corn or bread.
Smoke from the burning plant is toxic. When plant
material is used to roast f<?od over a frre, the poisonous           CERBERA ODALLAM (Pilikirbir) :
sap transferred to the food may be lethal.                     This plant is closely allied botanically to cerbera
                                                               thevetia. It is a small plant or a shrub that grows wild

## Cerbera Thevetia

                                                               all over India. The leaves are dark-green, fleshy and
    (see Plate 14, Fig. 84 and Plate 15, Fig. 89)
                                                               lanceolate, 20 to 30 em. long, and 4 to 6 em. broad.
    All parts of cerbera thevetia (yellow oleander;            The flowers are white, like those of jasmine. The
pila kaner) are poisonous. The flowers are large, bell-        fruit resembles a mango, is globular and dark-green
shaped and yellow, 5 to 7 cm.long and five em. wide,           and has a thick fibrous mesocarp which encloses
the five lobes spirally twisted and spreading, and the         usually a single seed. The seed is flattened and ovoid
leaves are lanceolate. The fruit is globular, light-green,     and contains two kernels which are pearly-white but
about 4 to 5 em. in diameter and contains a single nut         when dry it may have a bluish tinge or it may become
which is triangular with a deep groove along the edge.         gelatinous. Milky acrid juice (toxic) exudes from all
Each nut contains five pale yellow seeds. The seeds            parts of the plant. The active principles are cerberin,
contain four percent of the cardiac glycoside thevetin ,       cerberoside, odollin, odolotoxin, thevetin and cerapain
which is one-eighth as potent as ouabain and similar to        (glycosides).
digitalis in action; thevetoxin is similar to but less toxic
than thevetin; nerifolin (more potent than thevetin);
peruvoside, and ruvoside, cerberin and also a bitter
principle that acts on the CNS, and produces tetanoid
convulsions. All active principles are glycosides.
Milky juice exudes from all parts of the plant.
    Signs and Symptoms: The sap of the plant may
cause inflammation in sensitive individuals. Chewing
the bark or seed kernel causes a slight numbing
sensation and feeling of heat in the mouth and purging.
Ingestion causes burning pain in the mouth, dryness
of throat, tingling and numbness of tongue, vomiting,
diarrhoea, headache, giddiness, dilated pupils, loss of
muscular power and fainting. Pulse is rapid, weak and
irregular, blood pressure low. Heart block, collapse
and death from peripheral circulatory failure occurs.
    Fatal Dose: 8 to 10 seeds; 15 to 20 g. of root; 5
to 10 leaves.                                                              Fig. (35-2). Cerbera odallam.

## Asphyxiants



     Classification: Henderson and Haggard divided             lighter than air and significant quantities are present
noxious gases into five groups.                                only in the upper reaches in the vicinity of a fire.
     (1) IRRITANTS: The gases injure the air passages              In a closed environment, greater concentration
or lungs or both, and produce inflammatory changes.            is present at 1.5 to 2 metres above the ground. CO
They are smoke, tear gases, ammonia, formaldehyde,             seeps through openings around doors or large cracks
chlorine, phosgene, nitrogen dioxide, sulphur dioxide.
                                                               in walls. It may also rise through a ceiling crack into
     (2) CHEMICAL ASPHYXIANTS :These are gases
                                                               an attic or from a tight confined space, through plaster
which by combining with haemoglobin, or by acting on
some tissue constituent prevent oxygen from reaching           board or dry wall, into an adjacent area. In this way it
tissue or the tissues from using it. The examples are          may spread through an entire house and even apstair
carbon monoxide, hydrogen sulphide, arsine, carbon             rooms. A mixture of CO with 2Y2 times of its volume
disulphide, and cyanide.                                       of air is highly explosive in the presence of a flame.
     (3) SIMPLE ASPHYXIANTS : These are inert                      Action : CO is readily absorbed across the
gases, which when breathed in high concentration act           alveolus and combines with haemoglobin. Normal
mechanically by excluding oxygen. They are carbon              blood contains 21% of oxygen, with 18 to 19%
dioxide, methane, helium, nitrogen and nitrous oxide.          bound to haemoglobin and 2% dissolved in plasma.
Symptoms appear when these gases exceed 20 to 30%              About 10 to 21% CO is present in extracellular
of inspired air. When the inspired air contains less than
                                                               tissues combined with myoglobin and haemoproteins.
10% oxygen death may result from slight exertion.
                                                               CO affinity to myoglobin is about 40 times greater
     (4) VOLA TILE DRUGS : These are gases with little
or no irritant effect upon the air-passages, which act         than oxygen, which may cause direct myocardial
after absorption into the blood, either as an anaesthetic,     depression. CO has 200 to 300 times greater affinity
or as agents toxic to the liver, kidneys or other organs       for haemoglobin than that of oxygen. It displaces
including the nervous system. They are aliphatic               oxygen from its combination with haemoglobin
hydrocarbons, halogenated hydrocarbons and aromatic            and forms a relatively stable compound known as
hydrocarbons.                                                  carboxyhaemoglobin. It thus reduces the oxygen
     (5) SYSTEMIC POISONS : These are insecticides,            content of the blood, and hence that of the tissues.
arsine, stibine.                                               It acts as a chemical asphyxiant and produces death
                                                               due to anaemic anoxia. CO is a potent cellular toxin.

is produced due to incomplete combustion of carbon.            blocking cytochrome A 3 oxidase and cytochrome
It is insoluble in water. It burns with a blue flame. It is    P-450, and therefore intracellular respiration.

Table (36-1). Symptoms of CO poisoning
 COHb%           Symptoms
 0 to 10%        No appreciable symptoms.
 10 to 20%       Breathlessness on moderate exertion, mild headache, weakness.
 20 to 30%       Throbbing headache, irritability, emotional instability, disturbed judgement, defective memory
                 and rapid fatigue.
 30 to40%        Severe headache, nausea, vomiting, dizziness, dimness of vision, confusion. cherry-red colour.
 40 to 50%       Increasing confusion, sometimes hallucinations, severe ataxia, rapid respirations and collapse
                 with attempts at exertion. Symptoms resemble alcoholic intoxication.
 50 to 60%       Syncope or coma with intermittent convulsions, rapid respirations, tachycardia with a weak
                 pulse and pink or red discolouration of the skin.
 60 to 70%       Increasing depth of coma with incontinence of urine and faeces.
 70 to80%        Profound coma with depressed or absent reflexes, a weak thready pulse, shallow and irregular
                 respirations and death.
 Above SO%       Rapid death from respiratory arrest.

although poisoning results from absorption of                may not take place for 6 to 8 months. Mortality varies
a specific toxin from the alimentary canal. Cl.              from 25 to 100%.

## Poisonous Foods

                                                          (top) varies in colour from yellow to orange or red,
    Poisonous foods are those which contain poison        and is covered by warty scales. It contains an alkaloid
dervied from plants, animals and inorganic chemicals.
    LATHYRUS SATIVUS (kesari dhal): This
is a variety of pulse which grows under extreme
conditions of drought. It is a staple food for the
low income groups in some areas of Central India.
Consumption of L. sativus seeds in quantities
exceeding 30% of the total diet for more than six
months have been known to cause paralysis. The
overall incidence of the disease in the endemic area
is about 4%. Men are more susceptible than women.
The active neurotoxic principle is B(N) oxalyl amino-
alanine (BOAA), which is present as a free aminoacid
in the seed c?tyledons to the extent of about one
percent.
    Onset is of three types: (1) acute, (2) subacute,         Amanita muscaria                Amanita phalloides

## Glossary Of Legal Terms

AB INITIO : From the beginning.                               CAVEAT : Let him beware; a warning against certain
ACCOMPLICE : One who aids, abets or encourages                acts.
the main person in the commission of an unlawful act,         CIVIL CASE : A case between two parties in their
though he is not actually present.                            private capacity, e.g., money matters, property, etc.
ACTION : A proceeding in a Civil Court.                       CLAIM : A demand to pay.
AD DIEM : From the cause to the effect.                       CODE : A collection of laws in a jurisdiction or on a
AD VALORUM : According to the value.                          specific topic area arranged and indexed by subject,
ACTUS REUS : Actual physical act causing death.               with revisions added to reflect the law currently in
AD HOC : For this purpose.                                    force.
AFFIDAVIT : A written statement in the name of a              COGNISABLE OFFENCE : An offence, in which a
person, called the deponent by whom it is voluntarily         police officer can arrest a person without warrant from
signed and sworn to or affirmed.                              the Magistrate, e.g. rape, murder, robbery, etc.
AMICUS CURIAE : A person not a party to an action             COMMITTAL : The sending of a person to prison
may petition to file a brief to provide special information   generally for a short period or temporary purpose.
on matters of law.                                            COMMON LAW : Judge made law as opposed to
APPELLANT : The person who files an appeal.                   codified law, i.e., law passed by legislature.
APPELLEE : The party against whom an appeal is                COMPLAINANT : One who makes a complaint to
brought.                                                      justices.
ASSESSOR : One who assists the Court in trying a              CONTEMPT : A wilful disregard or disobedience of
scientific or technical question, but who has no voice in     Court's orders, as well as such conduct as tends to
the decision.                                                 bring the authority of the Court and the administration
BATTERY : Intentional and unauthorised physical               of law into disrepute, or in some manner impede due
contact with a person without his consent.                    administration of justice.
BINDING OVER : Requiring a person to enter into               CONTRACT : Agreement by two or more parties to
a recognisance to perform some necessary act, e.g.,           carry out some specific activity.
binding over a person to give evidence.                       CORPUS DEFICTI : That which has constituted a
BURDEN OF PROOF: The need or duty to establish                breach of law.
proof of the facts at trial.                                  CORPUS DELICT : That which has constituted a
CASE LAW :That part ofthe law formulated, developed           breach of law.
and based on common custom of country Gudge made              CRIME : A social harm which has been defmed and
law) and originally unwritten.                                made punishable by law.

CRIMINAL CASE : A case between two parties in the            IPSO FACTO: By the mere fact.
public interest, e.g. thefts, murder, assaults, etc. One     MALAFIDES: Bad faith.
party is the government through the police.                  MALFEASANCE : The doing of a civil or unlawful act.
CRIMINAL LIABILITY : Responsibility imposed when             MALICE :An act committed with intent to cause injury
a criminal statute, regulation or ordinance is violated.     or harm, without just cause or provocation.
CULPABLE: Blamable; censurable indicating a fault or         MALICE AFORETHOUGHT : The element of evil in
a breach of legal duty which is not necessarily criminal.    the crime of murder.
DAMAGE : The injury suffered by a party.                     MANSLAUGHTER: Unlawful homicide not amounting
DAMAGES : The amount of compensation payable to              to wilful murder.
a party to whom injury has .been done.                       MATERIAL EVIDENCE : Evidence relevant to the
DECREE : A document under the seal of a Court setting        case, having a legitimate bearing on the decision.
out the relief granted to a party or parties.                MENS REA : (guilty mind): An evil intention, or

personal or business information that injures the            MISADVENTURE : An accident or mischance,
reputation or prospects of another. Spoken defamation        unexpected and undesigned, arising out of lawful act.
is slander; written defamation is libel.                     MISDEMENOUR: Any crime or indictable offence not
DE FACTO: In fact.                                           amounting to felony, such as perjury, conspiracy, and

is filed.                                                    MORAL TURPITUDE: An act of baseness, vileness,

## Formulae For Estimation Of Stature

## Medical Certificate Of Cause Of Death

                                         Directions for completing the form

  NAME OF DECEASED : To be given in full. Do not use                    ACCIDENTAL OR VIOLENT DEATHS: Both the
  initials. If deceased is an infant, not yet named at time of     external cause and the nature of the injury are needed
  death, write 'Son of (S/o). or 'Daughter of (D/o ), Followed     and should be stated. The doctor or hospital should
  by names of mother and father.                                   always be able to describe the injury, stating the part
        AGE : If the deceased was over one year of age, give       of the body injured, and should give the external cause
  age in completed years. If the deceased was below I year of      in full when this is shown. Example: (a) Hypostatic
  age, give age in months and if below one month give age in       pneumonia; (b) Fracture of neck offemur; (c) Fall from
  completed number of days, and if below one day, in hours.        ladder at home.
        CAUSE OF DEATH : This part of the form should                   MATERNAL DEATHS: Be sure to answer the
  always be completed by the attending physician personally.       questions on pregnancy and delivery. This information
       The certificate of cause of death is divided into two       is needed for all women of child-bearing age, even though
  parts, I and II, Part I is again divided into three parts,       the pregnancy may have had nothing to do with the
  lines (a) (b) (c). If a single morbid condition completely       death.
  explains the death, then this will be written on line (a)             OLD AGE OR SENILITY : Old age (or senility)
  of part I, and nothing more need be written in the rest          should not be given as a cause of death if a more specific
  of Part I or in Part II, for example, smallpox, lobar            cause is known. If old age was a contributory factor,
  pneumonia, cardiac beriberi, are sufficient cause of             it should be entered in Part II. Example (a) chronic
  death and usually nothing more is needed.                        bronchitis, II old age.
       Often, however, a number of morbid conditions will               COMPLETENESS OF INFORMATION : A
  have been present at death, and the doctor must then             complete case history is not wanted, but, if the
  complete the certificate in the proper manner so that the        information is available, enough details should be given
  correct underlying cause will be tabulated. First, enter in      to enable the underlying cause to be properly clarified.
· Part I (a) the Immediate cause of death. This does not mean           EXAMPLE : Anaemia: Give type of anaemia,
  the mode of dying, e.g., heart failure, respiratory failure,     if known. Neoplasms: Indicate whether benign or
  etc., These terms should not appear on the certificate at all,   malignant, and site, with site of primary neoplasm,
  since they are modes of dying and not causes of death. Next      whenever possible, Heart disease : Describe the
  consider whether the immediate cause is a complication           condition specifically; if congestive heart failure,
  or delayed result of some other cause. If so, enter the          chronic cor pulmonale, etc., are mentioned; give
  antecedent cause in Part I, line (b). Sometimes there will       the antecedent conditions. Tetanus: Describe the
  be three stages in the course of events leading to death. If     antecedent injury, if known. Operation: State the
  so, line (c) will be completed. The underlying cause to be       condition for which the operation was performed.
  tabulated is always written last in Para I.                      Dysentry: Specify whether bacillary, amoebic, etc.,
       Morbid conditions or injuries may be present which          if known. Complications of pregnancy or delivery:
  were not directly related to the train of events causing         Describe the complication specifically. Tuberculosis :
  death, but which contributed in some way to the fatal            Give organs affected.
  outcome. Sometimes, the doctor finds it difficult to                  SYMPTOMATIC STATEMENT : Convulsions,
  decide, especially for infant deaths, which of several           diarrhoea, fever, ascites, jaundice, debility, etc., are
  independent conditions was the primary cause of death,           symptoms which may be due to any one of a number of
  but only one cause can be tabulated, so the doctor               different conditions. Sometimes nothing more is known,
  must decide. If the other diseases are not effects of the        but whenever possible, give the disease which caused the
  underlying cause, they are entered in Part II.                   symptom.
       Do not write two or more conditions on a single                  MANNER OF DEATH : Deaths not due to external
  line. Please write the names of the diseases (in full) in        cause should be identified as 'Natural'. If the cause of
  the certificate as legibly as possible to avoid the risk of      death is known, but it is not known whether it was the
  their being mi,sread.                                            result of an accident, suicide or homicide and is subject
       ONSET : Complete the column for interval between            to further investigation, the cause of death should
  onset and. death whenever possible, even if very                 invariably be filled in and the manner of death should
  appro~ately, e.g., "from birth" several years.                   be shown as 'Pending Investigation'.
